status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: Appointment scheduling SaaS with calendar sync, booking pages, team scheduling, payment collection, reminders, and CRM integration.

plan.description: Analyze the appointment scheduling SaaS market: identify top competitors (Calendly, Acuity, Cal.com), pricing benchmarks, feature gaps, and target segments (SMB vs enterprise, industry verticals).
acceptance_criteria: Competitor feature matrix covers at least 5 direct competitors
Market sizing includes TAM/SAM/SOM estimates with sources
Differentiators section identifies at least 3 defensible advantages
Pricing benchmarks documented across all major tiers

plan.description: Define business requirements and user stories for all core personas: host (scheduling owner), invitee (booker), team admin, and billing owner. Map end-to-end booking flow, cancellation/rescheduling, and team availability logic.
acceptance_criteria: User stories written for all 4 personas covering happy path and edge cases
Process flow diagrams cover booking, cancellation, rescheduling, and team scheduling
ROI analysis quantifies time-saved and no-show reduction metrics
All stories have testable acceptance criteria
Pricing tiers defined
Churn risk factors identified

plan.description: Build SaaS pricing model: freemium tier, individual pro, team, and enterprise plans. Model unit economics (CAC, LTV, churn), payment processing cost margins, and 24-month revenue projection.
acceptance_criteria: Pricing tiers clearly differentiated by feature set and user count
Unit economics model includes CAC, LTV, payback period, and gross margin
24-month revenue projection covers 3 scenarios (conservative, base, optimistic)
Payment processing fee margin analysis included for Stripe integration
MRR/ARR projections modeled
CAC and LTV estimated

plan.description: Draft Terms of Service, Privacy Policy (GDPR/CCPA compliant), Data Processing Agreement (DPA) for enterprise customers, and cookie policy. Review payment data handling obligations and CRM data sharing terms.
acceptance_criteria: ToS covers data ownership, liability limits, SLA commitments, and termination
Privacy Policy addresses GDPR Article 13 disclosure requirements
DPA template includes data sub-processor list and SCCs for EU transfers
Cookie policy categorizes all cookies and provides opt-out mechanism
Subscription terms cover cancellation and refunds
Data processing agreement drafted

plan.description: Conduct user research (5-8 interviews with target personas), define information architecture, and produce low-fidelity wireframes for: booking page, host dashboard, team scheduling setup, and payment configuration flows.
acceptance_criteria: At least 5 user interviews conducted and synthesized into key insights
Information architecture covers all primary user flows
Wireframes cover all 6 core screens at lo-fi fidelity
Accessibility audit against WCAG 2.1 AA checklist included

plan.description: Produce the Product Requirements Document (PRD) consolidating market research, business requirements, and UX findings. Define MVP scope, v1.0 roadmap, success metrics (activation rate, booking volume, churn), and prioritized backlog.
acceptance_criteria: PRD defines clear in-scope vs out-of-scope for MVP
Each roadmap item has owner, estimated effort, and success metric
Backlog items prioritized using RICE or similar framework
Success metrics are measurable and baselined

plan.description: Produce high-fidelity UI designs and interactive prototype for all MVP screens. Establish design system (color tokens, typography, spacing, component library). Validate with 3 usability tests.
acceptance_criteria: Hi-fi designs produced for all 9 screens with desktop and mobile viewports
Design system documents all tokens and reusable components
Interactive prototype covers end-to-end booking flow
3 usability tests conducted and critical issues resolved in designs

plan.description: Design relational database schema for scheduling_app: users, organizations, event_types, availability_rules, bookings, payments, reminders, calendar_connections, crm_integrations. Include indexes, constraints, and migration files.
acceptance_criteria: ERD covers all entities with cardinality annotations
All foreign keys have indexes; unique constraints enforced on email+org
Soft-delete pattern implemented for bookings and event types
Migration files are idempotent and reversible
Seed data includes representative fixtures for all entity types

plan.description: Design OpenAPI 3.1 spec for all scheduling_app endpoints: auth, event types, availability, bookings, calendar sync, payments, reminders, team management, CRM webhooks, and public booking API.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI spec validates with no errors against 3.1 schema
All endpoints have request/response schemas, error codes, and examples
Pagination defined for all list endpoints
Webhook payload schemas documented for calendar and CRM events
Rate limiting headers documented in spec

plan.description: Bootstrap scheduling_app monorepo: Next.js 14 frontend, FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL + Redis, Docker Compose for local dev, environment variable schema, pre-commit hooks, and linting/formatting config.
acceptance_criteria: `docker-compose up` starts all services with health checks passing
.env.example documents all required environment variables with descriptions
Pre-commit hooks run linting and type checks on staged files
Frontend and backend both have hot-reload working in dev mode
README includes first-run setup instructions

plan.description: Implement core scheduling engine: event type CRUD, availability rule calculation (recurring windows, date overrides, buffer times, max daily bookings), conflict detection, booking lifecycle (pending → confirmed → cancelled/rescheduled), and timezone handling.
acceptance_criteria: Availability engine correctly computes open slots across timezone boundaries
Conflict detection prevents double-bookings under concurrent requests (tested with load)
Buffer time before/after events correctly removed from available slots
Booking state machine enforces valid transitions only
All functions have unit tests with >90% coverage

plan.description: Implement calendar sync integrations: Google Calendar (OAuth2, two-way sync via push notifications), Microsoft Outlook (OAuth2, Graph API, delta sync), and iCal export. Handle token refresh, webhook receipt, and sync conflict resolution.
acceptance_criteria: Google Calendar OAuth flow completes and tokens stored encrypted at rest
Push notifications received and processed for Google Calendar changes within 30s
Outlook delta sync handles pagination and throttling per Graph API limits
iCal export generates valid RFC 5545 feed for any event type
Token refresh handled silently before expiry

plan.description: Implement payment collection via Stripe: Checkout Session creation, webhook processing (payment_intent.succeeded, charge.refunded), Connect for marketplace payouts, subscription billing for SaaS plans, and refund handling.
acceptance_criteria: Checkout session created and booking held until payment confirmed
Stripe webhook signature verified before processing any event
Failed payments trigger booking cancellation and invitee notification
Subscription plan changes (upgrade/downgrade) prorated correctly
Refund flow reverses payment and updates booking status atomically

plan.description: Implement reminder and notification system: email (SendGrid), SMS (Twilio), and in-app notifications. Support configurable reminder schedules (e.g., 24h + 1h before), no-show follow-ups, and cancellation/reschedule notifications.
acceptance_criteria: Email reminders delivered within 60s of scheduled time
SMS reminders fall back gracefully if Twilio delivery fails
Host and invitee both receive appropriate notifications for all trigger types
Reminder schedule configurable per event type (times, channels, templates)
Unsubscribe link functional and persisted in preferences

plan.description: Implement CRM integration layer: HubSpot (contacts, deals, activity log), Salesforce (leads, opportunities), and generic webhook outbound. Map booking events to CRM objects bidirectionally. Include field mapping configuration UI backend.
acceptance_criteria: HubSpot contact created/updated on every booking with correct field mapping
Salesforce integration handles OAuth and API version compatibility
Generic webhook includes HMAC signature and retry with exponential backoff
Field mapping configuration stored per-integration and editable via API
Failed CRM syncs logged to audit log with error detail and do not fail the booking

plan.description: Implement team scheduling features: round-robin assignment, collective scheduling (all team members must be available), managed event types with team ownership, team member availability aggregation, and host assignment logic.
acceptance_criteria: Round-robin assignment distributes bookings evenly, tracked in DB
Collective scheduling only shows slots where ALL assigned members are free
Team admin can add/remove members and assign event types
Availability gaps correctly propagated when any team member is unavailable
Load balancing respects max daily bookings per team member

plan.description: Build public booking page: embeddable iframe and hosted page. Shows event type info, available time slots, timezone selector, invitee form, and payment step. Must be SEO-friendly (SSR) and load under 2s on mobile.
acceptance_criteria: Booking page renders correct slots in invitee's local timezone
Payment step integrates Stripe Elements with 3DS support
Page passes Core Web Vitals (LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1, FID < 100ms)
Iframe embed works without CORS issues and is responsive
Confirmation page shows booking details and calendar add links (Google, iCal)

plan.description: Build host dashboard: event type management, booking list with filters, calendar view, availability settings, integration status (calendar, CRM, payments), reminder configuration, and analytics overview (bookings, revenue, no-shows).
acceptance_criteria: Event type editor validates all fields and previews public booking URL
Booking list supports filter by status, date range, event type, and invitee search
Calendar view shows all bookings with conflict highlighting
Availability rule builder supports recurring windows and date-specific overrides
Analytics dashboard shows 7/30/90 day trends for bookings and revenue

plan.description: Build team scheduling UI: team creation and member management, team event type assignment, collective vs round-robin mode toggle, per-member availability overrides, and team analytics breakdown.
acceptance_criteria: Team admin can invite members via email with role assignment
Scheduling mode switch (round-robin/collective) immediately previews slot availability change
Per-member availability overrides reflected in public booking page within 60s
Team analytics show per-member booking counts and load distribution

plan.description: Conduct security review: threat model for scheduling_app (STRIDE), OWASP Top 10 assessment, OAuth token storage audit, Stripe PCI-DSS scope validation, SQL injection and XSS scanning, and SOC 2 security control gap analysis.
acceptance_criteria: Threat model covers all trust boundaries including calendar OAuth and Stripe webhook
All High/Critical OWASP findings have remediation plans
OAuth tokens confirmed encrypted at rest (AES-256) and never logged
Stripe integration confirmed SAQ-A scope (no raw card data touches servers)
SOC 2 CC6 (logical access) control gaps documented with remediation owners

plan.description: Produce SOC 2 Type I readiness artifacts: control matrix mapping to Trust Services Criteria (CC, A, PI, C, P), policy documents (access control, incident response, change management, vendor management), evidence collection plan, and audit preparation checklist.

payload.standard: SOC 2 Type I (path to Type II)

acceptance_criteria: Control matrix maps every SOC 2 CC criterion to at least one implemented control
All 5 policy documents reviewed and approved by designated owner
Evidence collection plan identifies artifact type, owner, and collection cadence per control
Readiness assessment scores each criterion as implemented/partial/gap with remediation plan
No Critical gaps remain unaddressed in the readiness assessment

plan.description: Write unit and integration tests for all backend modules: scheduling engine (availability, conflict detection, timezone), calendar sync, payments (with Stripe test mode), reminders, CRM integrations, and team scheduling logic.

payload.mocking: pytest-mock for external APIs; Stripe test mode for payments

acceptance_criteria: Overall backend test coverage ≥85%
Availability engine has parametrized tests covering DST transitions and edge cases
Stripe payment flow tested with test mode covering success, failure, and refund paths
Calendar sync tests use recorded VCR cassettes for Google and Outlook API calls
All tests pass in CI with no flaky tests (verified over 5 consecutive runs)

plan.description: Write frontend unit tests (Vitest + Testing Library) for all booking page and dashboard components, and E2E tests (Playwright) covering: full booking flow with payment, team booking round-robin, calendar sync trigger, and cancellation flow.
acceptance_criteria: Frontend component coverage ≥80%
All 5 E2E scenarios pass against staging environment
Booking page E2E verified on Chrome, Firefox, and mobile Safari viewports
Payment E2E uses Stripe test cards for success and decline scenarios
E2E tests run in under 10 minutes in CI

plan.description: Set up CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions): lint/typecheck, unit tests, integration tests, Docker build, staging deploy (Railway or Render), E2E tests against staging, and production deploy gate with manual approval. Add Datadog monitoring and PagerDuty alerting.
acceptance_criteria: CI pipeline runs on every PR and completes in under 15 minutes
Staging deploy is fully automated on merge to main
Production deploy requires manual approval gate in GitHub Actions
Datadog monitors p50/p95/p99 latency for /bookings and /availability endpoints
PagerDuty alert fires within 5 minutes of availability endpoint error rate exceeding 1%

plan.description: Define SLA commitments, write operational runbooks for all critical failure modes (calendar sync outage, Stripe webhook lag, reminder delivery failure, DB failover), and set up incident response process with severity levels and escalation paths.
acceptance_criteria: SLA document defines uptime calculation methodology and exclusions
Each runbook includes detection, diagnosis, mitigation, and post-mortem steps
Incident severity matrix maps symptoms to severity level (P0-P3) unambiguously
Escalation path documented for each severity level with contact owners
All runbooks linked from Datadog dashboard for on-call reference

plan.description: Build analytics data pipeline: capture booking events to an analytics schema (booking funnel, drop-off points, revenue by event type, no-show rates). Implement dashboard API endpoints and CSV export for host analytics reports.
acceptance_criteria: All 6 core metrics computed correctly against test dataset
Analytics endpoints return data within 500ms for up to 10k bookings
CSV export includes all booking fields and is importable to Excel/Sheets
Materialized views refreshed on a schedule (hourly) without blocking booking writes
Funnel drop-off correctly attributes abandonment at each booking step

plan.description: Implement AI-powered scheduling assistant using the coordinator pattern: booking summary agent (summarizes invitee context from CRM before meeting), smart availability optimizer (suggests optimal booking windows based on host preferences + historical patterns), and no-show prediction agent (flags high-risk bookings for proactive outreach).
acceptance_criteria: Booking brief generated within 10s using CRM contact history and past booking data
Availability optimizer returns ranked slot suggestions with reasoning
No-show predictor achieves >70% precision on historical test data
Coordinator routes to correct specialist agent for each request type
All agent outputs logged to audit trail with trace_id

plan.description: Write developer documentation: API reference (auto-generated from OpenAPI spec), integration guides (Google Calendar, Outlook, Stripe, HubSpot, Salesforce), embed guide for booking page, webhook event catalog, and architecture decision records (ADRs).
acceptance_criteria: API reference auto-generated from OpenAPI spec with all examples rendered
Each integration guide includes step-by-step OAuth setup with screenshots
Webhook event catalog documents all event types with example payloads
Embed guide includes iframe snippet, query parameter reference, and CSS override docs
At least 5 ADRs document key architectural decisions (DB choice, sync strategy, payment scope)

flaws: Step 11 mandates concurrent conflict detection but specifies no locking strategy. PostgreSQL advisory locks, row-level locks, and optimistic concurrency with version columns all have different failure modes under load. Without specifying which approach, two concurrent bookings of the same slot will race. This is the #1 production failure mode in scheduling systems and the plan hand-waves it.

Step 12 omits Google Calendar push notification channel renewal. Channels expire every 7 days and must be renewed before expiry or the sync silently stops. No keepalive job, no channel registry, no expiry tracking. This will cause calendar sync outages starting on day 8 of production.

Step 13 introduces Stripe Connect for marketplace payouts with zero acknowledgment of KYC/onboarding complexity. Express and Custom Connect accounts require identity verification flows, payout schedule configuration, and Stripe's own review process. This is a multi-week integration, not a line item.

pytz is deprecated since Python 3.9 in favor of zoneinfo (stdlib). Step 11 specifies 'python-dateutil + pytz'. Using pytz for future DST transitions in 2025+ timezone data is a reliability risk; IANA database updates via tzdata package are required and not mentioned.

Step 17 (booking page with PaymentStep component) has no dependency on Step 13 (Stripe payments backend). The dependency graph is broken — frontend payment integration cannot be built without the backend payment API.

Step 27 no-show predictor requires >70% precision but there is zero historical booking data at launch. Acceptance criteria demand ML model validation against 'historical test data' that does not exist. This step cannot pass its own criteria at launch.

Multi-tenant data isolation is absent from the schema design (Step 8). The schema has organizations table but no PostgreSQL Row-Level Security policies or schema-per-tenant strategy. A misconfigured query at the ORM layer can leak one org's bookings to another — no structural guardrail prevents this.

OAuth token refresh race condition is unaddressed across Steps 12 and 15. Multiple concurrent API requests with an expired token will all attempt refresh simultaneously, invalidating each other's refresh tokens. No distributed lock around token refresh is specified.

Step 14 uses Celery + Redis for reminder scheduling but specifies no Redis HA strategy. If Redis restarts, all scheduled reminder tasks are lost. No mention of Redis persistence (AOF/RDB), Sentinel, or task result backend durability.

Feature gating between subscription tiers (defined in Step 6, billed in Step 13) has no implementation step. There is no middleware, permission layer, or entitlement service connecting a user's active Stripe subscription plan to which features they can access.

Step 9 documents rate limiting headers in the OpenAPI spec but no implementation step adds rate limiting to the API. Rate limiting on /bookings and /availability endpoints is critical for preventing abuse of the public booking page.

Google Calendar OAuth requires app verification by Google for the calendar.events scope before public launch. This is a multi-week review process with a required OAuth consent screen review. It is entirely absent from the plan and will block production launch.
suggestions: Add an explicit locking strategy to Step 11: define a bookings.slot_lock advisory lock key pattern or a booking_holds table with TTL (e.g., 10 min) to hold a slot during payment. Include a cleanup job for expired holds. Test with at least 50 concurrent booking attempts to the same slot.

Add a calendar_channel_registry table in Step 8 and a scheduled job (every 6 days) in Step 12 to renew Google Calendar push notification channels before they expire. Include a dead-channel detector that falls back to polling.

Scope Stripe Connect explicitly in Step 13: decide on Standard vs Express vs Custom accounts now. Standard is 80% simpler and covers most use cases. Document this as an ADR. If marketplace payouts are MVP, budget 3-4 weeks for Stripe Connect onboarding flows alone.

Replace pytz with zoneinfo + tzdata in Step 11. Change the acceptance criteria to explicitly test a booking made today for a slot 6 months in the future across a DST boundary (e.g., America/New_York in March).
Add dependency edge from Step 13 to Step 17. Also add Step 12 (calendar sync) as a dependency of Step 17 since the confirmation page shows 'Add to Google Calendar' links that require the OAuth flow.

Defer Step 27 (AI agents) or replace the precision acceptance criterion with 'generates booking brief from available CRM data within 10s' — drop the no-show predictor until 6 months of production data exists. Ship the summarizer first.
Add PostgreSQL RLS policies to Step 8 as a first-class deliverable: CREATE POLICY per table scoped to organization_id. Test with a cross-tenant query in integration tests that must return 0 rows.
Add a token_refresh_lock Redis key per calendar connection in Step 12. Only one process may hold the lock; others must wait and re-read the refreshed token rather than retrying refresh.

Add Redis persistence configuration (AOF enabled, fsync everysec) and a Celery task result backend (PostgreSQL or Redis with persistence) to Step 14. Document RPO for reminder delivery: acceptable to lose reminders scheduled within the last N seconds of a Redis restart.

Add a feature entitlement step (or expand Step 10) defining a plan_features table and a middleware decorator (@require_plan('team')) that checks the org's active Stripe subscription. Wire Stripe webhook subscription.updated to update the org's plan in DB.

Implement application-level rate limiting in Step 10 bootstrap using slowapi (FastAPI middleware): 60 req/min on public booking endpoints, 10 req/min on /auth/. Add to docker-compose as nginx or rely on Railway's native rate limiting — pick one and document it.

Add Google OAuth app verification to the project roadmap between Steps 12 and 24. Submit for review immediately after Step 12 is complete. Do not plan a public launch until verification is approved — plan 4-6 weeks.
missing: Email deliverability setup: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records for SendGrid sending domain. Without these, booking confirmations and reminders will hit spam folders at Gmail and Outlook. Zero mention in Steps 14 or 24.

Webhook idempotency: Stripe and calendar webhooks can deliver duplicate events. No idempotency key table or processed_event_ids deduplication step is specified. Double-processing a payment.succeeded event could double-confirm a booking.

GDPR right to erasure implementation: Step 4 creates a Privacy Policy promising erasure rights, but there is no implementation step for a data deletion workflow (cascade delete user data, anonymize booking history, propagate deletion to CRM integrations).

Database connection pooling: FastAPI + PostgreSQL at production load requires PgBouncer or asyncpg connection pooling. Missing from Step 10 bootstrap and Step 24 infra. Will cause connection exhaustion under moderate load.

Database backup and recovery: No PITR (Point-in-Time Recovery), no automated snapshot schedule, no RTO/RPO targets. Step 25 has a db_failover runbook but no underlying backup infrastructure to execute against.

Booking slug enumeration protection: Public booking pages need non-guessable slugs (UUID or random string) and rate limiting on the public route. Sequential or name-based slugs allow scraping all hosted calendars.

iCal feed authentication: Step 12 generates iCal feeds but no mention of whether they are public or token-authenticated. Unauthenticated feeds leak host booking data (invitee names, meeting topics) to anyone with the URL.

Celery worker scaling specification: Step 14 specifies Celery + Redis but never defines how workers scale independently from the API tier. At high booking volume, reminder workers queue up. No autoscaling policy or worker count baseline.

Content Security Policy for iframe embed: Step 17 requires embed support but CSP headers that block iframe embedding are a common production surprise. frame-ancestors policy needs explicit configuration and testing against common embed targets (Wordpress, Squarespace, Webflow).

Materialized view refresh strategy for analytics (Step 26): REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW blocks concurrent reads by default unless CONCURRENTLY is used, which requires a unique index. Neither is specified. Hourly refresh on a large bookings table will cause analytics query timeouts.
security_risks: SSRF via generic webhook outbound (Step 15): The outbound webhook URL is user-configurable. Without an allowlist or URL validation layer, a malicious user can point it at http://169.254.169.254 (AWS IMDS) or internal network services. The plan specifies HMAC signing of outbound payloads but says nothing about validating the destination URL.

OAuth token key management: Step 12 specifies tokens 'encrypted at rest (AES-256)' but not where the encryption key lives. If it is in an environment variable alongside the data, it provides no protection against a DB dump. KMS (AWS KMS, GCP Cloud KMS) or a secrets manager is required — not mentioned.

Open redirect in OAuth callback: OAuth2 callback handling for Google and Outlook is a well-known vector for open redirect attacks if the state parameter or redirect_uri is not validated server-side. Not mentioned in Step 20's OWASP assessment scope.

Stripe webhook timestamp validation missing: Step 13 specifies signature verification but Stripe webhooks should also reject events with timestamps older than 5 minutes (replay window). The plan omits this check, leaving a replay window open.

Booking page slug enumeration: If booking page slugs are derived from user names or are sequential integers, an attacker can enumerate all hosts. Combined with calendar availability data (public by design), this leaks host schedules to scrapers. No mention of anti-enumeration measures.

JWT secret rotation: Step 9 specifies JWT auth but no mention of secret rotation strategy, token revocation list, or short expiry + refresh token pattern. A leaked JWT with a long TTL cannot be invalidated without a revocation mechanism.
final_review.summary: This is an above-average build plan with comprehensive feature coverage, clear acceptance criteria, and good separation of concerns between MVP and v1. However, it has three categories of critical gaps that will cause production failures: (1) concurrency — the booking conflict detection, OAuth token refresh race, and Celery task durability are all specified at the 'we will handle this' level without concrete mechanisms, which historically means they are not handled; (2) third-party integration operational realities — Google Calendar push channel renewal, Google OAuth app verification timelines, and Stripe Connect KYC complexity are each multi-week blockers that are invisible in the current plan; (3) data isolation and security fundamentals — no Row-Level Security, no SSRF protection on outbound webhooks, no key management strategy for OAuth tokens. The AI agents step (27) has acceptance criteria that are impossible to meet at launch. Scoring 63 for an MVP: the feature decomposition and documentation artifacts are solid, but the concurrency and integration gaps are concrete failure modes, not theoretical risks. A developer following this plan faithfully will ship a product that silently stops syncing calendars on day 8, leaks tokens under concurrent load, and blocks launch waiting for Google OAuth verification.

flaws: Step 11 mandates concurrent conflict detection but specifies no locking strategy. PostgreSQL advisory locks, row-level locks, and optimistic concurrency with version columns all have different failure modes under load. Without specifying which approach, two concurrent bookings of the same slot will race. This is the #1 production failure mode in scheduling systems and the plan hand-waves it.

Step 12 omits Google Calendar push notification channel renewal. Channels expire every 7 days and must be renewed before expiry or the sync silently stops. No keepalive job, no channel registry, no expiry tracking. This will cause calendar sync outages starting on day 8 of production.

Step 13 introduces Stripe Connect for marketplace payouts with zero acknowledgment of KYC/onboarding complexity. Express and Custom Connect accounts require identity verification flows, payout schedule configuration, and Stripe's own review process. This is a multi-week integration, not a line item.

pytz is deprecated since Python 3.9 in favor of zoneinfo (stdlib). Step 11 specifies 'python-dateutil + pytz'. Using pytz for future DST transitions in 2025+ timezone data is a reliability risk; IANA database updates via tzdata package are required and not mentioned.

Step 17 (booking page with PaymentStep component) has no dependency on Step 13 (Stripe payments backend). The dependency graph is broken — frontend payment integration cannot be built without the backend payment API.

Step 27 no-show predictor requires >70% precision but there is zero historical booking data at launch. Acceptance criteria demand ML model validation against 'historical test data' that does not exist. This step cannot pass its own criteria at launch.

Multi-tenant data isolation is absent from the schema design (Step 8). The schema has organizations table but no PostgreSQL Row-Level Security policies or schema-per-tenant strategy. A misconfigured query at the ORM layer can leak one org's bookings to another — no structural guardrail prevents this.

OAuth token refresh race condition is unaddressed across Steps 12 and 15. Multiple concurrent API requests with an expired token will all attempt refresh simultaneously, invalidating each other's refresh tokens. No distributed lock around token refresh is specified.

Step 14 uses Celery + Redis for reminder scheduling but specifies no Redis HA strategy. If Redis restarts, all scheduled reminder tasks are lost. No mention of Redis persistence (AOF/RDB), Sentinel, or task result backend durability.

Feature gating between subscription tiers (defined in Step 6, billed in Step 13) has no implementation step. There is no middleware, permission layer, or entitlement service connecting a user's active Stripe subscription plan to which features they can access.

Step 9 documents rate limiting headers in the OpenAPI spec but no implementation step adds rate limiting to the API. Rate limiting on /bookings and /availability endpoints is critical for preventing abuse of the public booking page.

Google Calendar OAuth requires app verification by Google for the calendar.events scope before public launch. This is a multi-week review process with a required OAuth consent screen review. It is entirely absent from the plan and will block production launch.

Materialized view refresh strategy for analytics (Step 26): REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW blocks concurrent reads by default unless CONCURRENTLY is used, which requires a unique index. Neither is specified. Hourly refresh on a large bookings table will cause analytics query timeouts.
security_risks: SSRF via generic webhook outbound (Step 15): The outbound webhook URL is user-configurable. Without an allowlist or URL validation layer, a malicious user can point it at http://169.254.169.254 (AWS IMDS) or internal network services. The plan specifies HMAC signing of outbound payloads but says nothing about validating the destination URL.

OAuth token key management: Step 12 specifies tokens 'encrypted at rest (AES-256)' but not where the encryption key lives. If it is in an environment variable alongside the data, it provides no protection against a DB dump. KMS (AWS KMS, GCP Cloud KMS) or a secrets manager is required — not mentioned.

Open redirect in OAuth callback: OAuth2 callback handling for Google and Outlook is a well-known vector for open redirect attacks if the state parameter or redirect_uri is not validated server-side. Not mentioned in Step 20's OWASP assessment scope.

Stripe webhook timestamp validation missing: Step 13 specifies signature verification but Stripe webhooks should also reject events with timestamps older than 5 minutes (replay window). The plan omits this check, leaving a replay window open.

Booking page slug enumeration: If booking page slugs are derived from user names or are sequential integers, an attacker can enumerate all hosts. Combined with calendar availability data (public by design), this leaks host schedules to scrapers. No mention of anti-enumeration measures.

JWT secret rotation: Step 9 specifies JWT auth but no mention of secret rotation strategy, token revocation list, or short expiry + refresh token pattern. A leaked JWT with a long TTL cannot be invalidated without a revocation mechanism.
history.summary: This is an above-average build plan with comprehensive feature coverage, clear acceptance criteria, and good separation of concerns between MVP and v1. However, it has three categories of critical gaps that will cause production failures: (1) concurrency — the booking conflict detection, OAuth token refresh race, and Celery task durability are all specified at the 'we will handle this' level without concrete mechanisms, which historically means they are not handled; (2) third-party integration operational realities — Google Calendar push channel renewal, Google OAuth app verification timelines, and Stripe Connect KYC complexity are each multi-week blockers that are invisible in the current plan; (3) data isolation and security fundamentals — no Row-Level Security, no SSRF protection on outbound webhooks, no key management strategy for OAuth tokens. The AI agents step (27) has acceptance criteria that are impossible to meet at launch. Scoring 63 for an MVP: the feature decomposition and documentation artifacts are solid, but the concurrency and integration gaps are concrete failure modes, not theoretical risks. A developer following this plan faithfully will ship a product that silently stops syncing calendars on day 8, leaks tokens under concurrent load, and blocks launch waiting for Google OAuth verification.
